meta.title: C219 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes CLS C219 engine — M272, M113, M273, M156, OM642. Real UK costs, the balance shaft warning, and honest reliability data for the four‑door coupé pioneer. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes CLS C219 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned C219 engine typically costs £1,800–£10,000 supply only, depending on engine — the volume OM642 diesel sits at £2,200–£4,500, while the M156 AMG V8 ranges up to £10,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes C219 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 V6 diesel (CLS 320 CDI / CLS 350 CDI) — proven in the W211 E‑Class and capable of 200,000+ miles with routine servicing. The M113 V8 (CLS 500) is also a strong performer, free of the M156's camshaft adjuster issues and the M272's balance shaft concerns.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes CLS C219?

acceptedAnswer.text: Balance shaft sprocket wear on the M272 V6 petrol, oil cooler seal leaks on the OM642 diesel, camshaft adjuster and tappet wear on the M156 AMG V8, supercharger clutch and heat‑related wear on the M113 AMG V8, and timing chain tensioner wear on the M113 and M273 V8s.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes CLS C219 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine and maintenance history. The OM642 diesel and M113 V8 are the two most reliable options. The M272 V6 carries a known balance shaft risk — check history before buying. The M156 AMG V8 is a masterpiece but demands meticulous maintenance and carries high repair costs.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes C219 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but a high‑mileage M272 V6 (CLS 350 / CLS 350 CGI) without documented balance shaft history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected M156 (CLS 63 AMG) with ticking noises is the second. An OM642 diesel with visible oil leaks around the engine bay should also be approached with caution.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my C219 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Check engine light with camshaft position codes (M272), oil leaks from the engine bay (OM642), ticking noise on cold start (M156), supercharger clutch noise or loss of boost (M113), or timing chain rattle (M113/M273) are the key warning signs. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes CLS C219 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well‑kept £7,000 C219, a £4,000 OM642 recon is 57% of the car's value — borderline but viable. Against a tired £4,000 C219, the same recon is over 100% — walk away. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line. For a well‑maintained CLS 63 AMG with values above £15,000, a £10,000 M156 recon may still make sense.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes C219 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 diesel and M113 V8 can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing. The M272's limiting factor is the balance shaft — addressed proactively, it can reach 150,000+ miles. The M156's camshaft adjusters are the limiting factor — repaired, it's a durable engine but costs escalate. The M113 AMG supercharged engine is capable of high mileage but heat‑related wear demands attention.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the C219, C218 and C257?

acceptedAnswer.text: All three are CLS‑Class generations. The C219 is the first‑generation four‑door coupé (this page, 2004–2010), the C218 is the second generation (2010–2018), and the C257 is the third generation (2018–2023). The C219 was the pioneer of the four‑door coupé segment and is the only generation based on the W211 E‑Class platform. Costs vary by generation — see each model's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C219 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate, in the vehicle documentation, and on the engine block itself. The 8th character of the VIN often indicates the engine variant. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes CLS C219 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the first‑generation four‑door coupé — from the M272 V6 petrol to the M113 and M156 V8s and the OM642 V6 diesel. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the AMG‑tuned engines that made this the pioneer of the four‑door coupé segment. Backed by 1,250+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes CLS C219 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C219 four‑door coupé specifically — including the pre‑ and post‑facelift engine changes that define this generation's ownership story. Based on the W211 E‑Class platform, sharing major components including engines and transmissions.

overview.intro: The C219 is the first‑generation CLS‑Class, launched in October 2004 as the pioneer of the four‑door coupé segment. Based on the W211 E‑Class platform and sharing major components including engines and transmissions, it introduced a fastback body style that combined coupé styling with saloon practicality. Designed by Michael Fink, the C219 was produced until July 2010. A mid‑cycle update in 2006 brought new engines including the M273 V8 and M156 AMG V8, replacing the earlier M113 units.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: W211 (Mercedes‑Benz E‑Class platform) • Predecessor: None (CLS‑Class launched as new model) • Successor: C218 (second‑generation CLS‑Class) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (C219 was the only body style)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The C219 CLS generated significant UK engine enquiry demand in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM642 diesel and M272 petrol variants as these cars enter their second decade of service.

items.quote: “The 3.0‑litre V6 turbodiesel is the C219's most dependable engine — proven in the W211 E‑Class and capable of 200,000+ miles with routine servicing. The 350 CDI's 268 PS version delivers effortless performance without the AMG V8's running costs.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want diesel economy and six‑figure mileage potential.

“The 5.0‑litre M113 is the C219's most robust V8 — smooth, torquey, and free of the M156's camshaft adjuster issues. 306 PS and 460 Nm deliver effortless performance without the supercharged AMG's complexity.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want V8 refinement without AMG running costs.

“The M272's balance shaft sprocket wear is this generation's defining risk — a design flaw that can lead to catastrophic engine failure. Mercedes acknowledged the issue but didn't extend the warranty as widely as with later engines.” Who it’s for: Buyers who understand the risk and have documented balance shaft history.

“The 6.2‑litre naturally aspirated V8 is a masterpiece — but punishing to repair. Camshaft adjuster wear, tappet noise, and the entire engine's high‑strung nature add up fast. A full replacement runs well into five figures.” Who it’s for: AMG enthusiasts with deep pockets and full service history.

“The entry‑level diesel shares the same robust OM642 architecture as the more powerful 350 CDI — but costs less to buy and less to replace. Oil cooler seals and swirl flaps are the only real demands.” Who it’s for: Buyers who want diesel economy on a tighter budget.

“Often overshadowed by the later M156, the supercharged 5.4‑litre M113 is actually more reliable — fewer camshaft issues, proven supercharger, and a more robust bottom end. 476 PS is still plenty in 2026.” Who it’s for: AMG buyers who want performance without the M156's anxiety.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — budget for balance shaft work

rows.commonMajorFailure: Timing chain tensioner / valve cover gaskets

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best AMG — more reliable than M156

rows.commonMajorFailure: Oil cooler seal leaks / swirl flaps

economicsRule.title: The C219 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,000 OM642 recon against a £7,000 C219 sits at 57% — that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold. Against a tired £4,000 C219, the same recon is over 100% — walk away. A £5,000 M113 recon against a well‑kept £10,000 CLS 500 is 50% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C219 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: OM642 (CLS 320 CDI) — cheap oil cooler seal maintenance, proven V6 diesel.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C219 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M156 (CLS 63 AMG) — camshaft adjuster wear, tappet issues, and V8 complexity add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which C219 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M113 (CLS 500) — V8 refinement without the M156's anxiety or the M272's balance shaft risk.

problems.title: M272 Balance Shaft Sprocket Wear (CLS 280 / CLS 350 / CLS 350 CGI)
problems.rootCause: The M272 V6's balance shaft sprocket has a design flaw — the sprocket wears prematurely, causing timing chain slack, camshaft position sensor codes, and eventual engine failure if ignored. Mercedes acknowledged the issue but did not issue a recall.
problems.recommendation: If your C219 CLS 350 develops a check engine light with camshaft position codes, get the balance shaft inspected immediately — a £1,500–£3,500 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £3,000–£5,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare M272 engine prices for your C219 CLS 350 →

problems.title: OM642 Oil Cooler Seal Leaks
problems.rootCause: The OM642 V6 diesel's oil cooler seals degrade over time, causing oil leaks into the 'V' of the engine. This can lead to oil contamination of the engine bay, belt failure, and eventually engine damage if ignored.
problems.recommendation: If your C219 diesel develops an oil smell or visible oil in the engine bay, get the oil cooler seals inspected immediately — a £600–£1,500 repair is far cheaper than the £2,200–£4,500 replacement that follows a neglected leak.

cta.label: Compare OM642 engine prices for your C219 CLS 320 CDI →

problems.title: M156 Camshaft Adjuster & Tappet Wear (CLS 63 AMG)
problems.rootCause: The M156's camshaft adjuster mechanism and hydraulic tappets wear prematurely, causing a characteristic ticking noise on start‑up, reduced power, and check engine lights. The issue is well‑documented across the M156 engine family.
problems.recommendation: If your CLS 63 AMG develops a ticking noise on cold start, get the camshaft adjusters inspected immediately — a £1,500–£3,500 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £5,500–£10,000 replacement that follows a failed adjuster.

cta.label: Compare M156 engine prices for your C219 CLS 63 AMG →

problems.title: M113 Supercharger Clutch & Heat‑Related Wear (CLS 55 AMG)
problems.rootCause: The M113 supercharged V8 generates significant heat in the engine bay — the supercharger clutch can fail, and valve cover gaskets and cooling system components degrade prematurely. The supercharger itself is robust but the clutch mechanism is a known weak point.
problems.recommendation: If you own a CLS 55 AMG, treat supercharger clutch and cooling system inspections as annual maintenance — £800–£2,000 now is far cheaper than the £4,500–£8,000 replacement that follows a neglected supercharger failure.

cta.label: Compare M113 engine prices for your C219 CLS 55 AMG →
